rule.desc: Enforce immutability through type hints (e.g., `Sequence`, `Mapping`, `AbstractSet`) rather than creating immutable copies of collections.
rule.when_to_use: - Return types: Use `Sequence[T]` instead of `list[T]` when callers shouldn't modify - Return types: Use `Mapping[K, V]` instead of `dict[K, V]` when callers shouldn't modify - Return types: Use `AbstractSet[T]` instead of `set[T]` when callers shouldn't modify - Parameters: Use `Sequence[T]` for flexible input (accepts list, tuple, etc.) - Parameters: Use `Mapping[K, V]` for flexible input (accepts dict, etc.) - Parameters: Use `AbstractSet[T]` for flexible input (accepts set, frozenset, etc.)
rule.rationale: Type hints provide compile-time immutability guarantees without runtime overhead. Creating immutable copies (e.g., `tuple(my_list)`, `frozenset(my_set)`) adds unnecessary performance costs. Static type checking via mypy enforces immutability contracts.

example.code:
# Good - immutability enforced via type hint def get_categories() -> Sequence[str]: """Returns immutable view of categories.""" categories = ["Food", "Transport", "Entertainment"] return categories # Returns mutable list, but type says immutable
# Callers cannot modify (enforced by mypy) cats = get_categories() # cats.append("New") # mypy error: Sequence has no append method

# Good - immutable mapping def get_config() -> Mapping[str, int]: """Returns immutable config.""" config = {"timeout": 30, "retries": 3} return config # Returns mutable dict, typed as immutable
# Callers cannot modify cfg = get_config() # cfg["timeout"] = 60 # mypy error: Mapping doesn't support item assignment

# Good - immutable set def get_valid_currencies() -> AbstractSet[str]: """Returns immutable set of valid currencies.""" currencies = {"USD", "EUR", "GBP", "JPY"} return currencies # Returns mutable set, typed as immutable
# Callers cannot modify valid = get_valid_currencies() # valid.add("CHF") # mypy error: AbstractSet has no add method

# Bad - unnecessary runtime overhead def get_categories() -> tuple[str, ...]: """Returns immutable tuple.""" categories = ["Food", "Transport", "Entertainment"] return tuple(categories) # Runtime cost of creating tuple!

# Bad - promises mutability but shouldn't def get_categories() -> list[str]: """Returns categories.""" return ["Food", "Transport", "Entertainment"]
# Callers can modify (probably unintended) cats = get_categories() cats.append("New") # Works but might break assumptions!

rule.tool_support: mypy enforces method availability based on type. `Sequence`, `Mapping`, and `AbstractSet` don't have mutation methods.

notes.note: Use `from collections.abc import Set as AbstractSet` to avoid conflicts with the built-in `set` type.
